title: Test approach for rising tone state

content.text:
In @src/liblex.zig discuss with me the approach to write only the test "expect State.add apply rising tone for valid cases".

- Take @specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md as the source of truth.
- Take existing tests for code pattern, technique and convention.
- Don't change the test name.
- Focus the cases to the scan result composition in the specs.
- Only apply rising tone, this mean the existing word doesn't have any tone other than level.

Come up with the approach, then consult the Oracle for another perspective, but prevent the Oracle from producing any example code. You should not write any code.

content.thinking:
**Considering approach without code**

I need to discuss an approach that avoids using code. First, I should inspect the files as per the guidelines. Since AGENTS indicates I should confirm before executing any plans or actions, I’ll stick to that. The user is looking to come up with an approach to consult an oracle without executing code. I'll read the relevant source and specs, using shell commands without tools like rg since it's not installed. I can use sed or grep instead. After this, I'll consult the oracle while preventing example code execution. Also, maybe a commentary update would be useful!

content.text: I’ll inspect the spec and nearby tests to derive a test-only approach, then ask Oracle for critique without code examples.

input.command: grep -n "expect State.add apply rising tone for valid cases\|State.add apply.*tone\|rising tone\|tone" src/liblex.zig

result.output:
# Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method

The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.

## Vietnamese Telex Rules

### Literal input characters

- When any of the follow characters added to the word, change to literal input from the new character position: `F`, `J`, `W` (ignore cases), because these characters don't appear in formal Vietnamese spelling. `Z` existing in Vietnamese word such as _Dzũng_, _Hồ Dzếnh_
- Literal input remains until the new word.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
- This rule is lower priority than diacritics and tones rules.

### Diacritics

Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
Tones have a separate section.

#### Empty

- Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
- Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
  characters.

#### Circumflex

- Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.

#### Breve

- Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.

#### Horn

- Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.

#### Stroke

- Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.

#### Apply rules

- Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
- _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
  existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
  - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
  - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
- _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
  valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
  - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
- The existing character keep its case, tone.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Override rules

- New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
  - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
- Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
- Keep tone untouched.
- The existing character keep its case, tone.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Cancel rules

- The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
  produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
- The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
  - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
  - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
  - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
  - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
- The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
- Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
  remains until the new word.
  - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Auto-fill rules

- The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
  character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
- Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
  `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
  - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
  - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
- The new input character is appended literally.
- Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.

### Tones

Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
These vowel could take any tone.

#### Level (ngang)

- The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
  corresponding variants.
- If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.

#### Rising (sắc)

- Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
  -> `Ớ`.

#### Falling (huyền)

- Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
  -> `Ờ`.

#### Dipping-rising (hỏi)

- Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
  `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.

#### Rising-glottalized (ngã)

- Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
  -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.

#### Falling-glottalized (nặng)

- Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
  -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.

#### Apply rules

- Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
  position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
- If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.

#### Cancel rules

- Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
  the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
- If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
  position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Reset rules

- Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
  change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
  any tone (level), append the new character literally.
- If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
  position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Override rules

- Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
  from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
  the word. The override tone is always successful.

#### Scan pseudo-word

- Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
  the tone in the correct position.
- The existing characters must not be empty.
- Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
- If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
  character to scan, then stop.
- If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
  backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
- After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
  `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
  `<consonant><vowels>`.

#### Tone position rules

The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:

- Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
  tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
- Exceptions:
  - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
- If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
- If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
  - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
  - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
    prioritize the first to the last in the list.
  - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
    `U` count as part of the consonant.
  - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
  - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
    consonants, put on second vowel.
  - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
- Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
- Could not put tone for other cases.

content.thinking:
**Looking into implementation details**

I need to read up on the `liblex` in relation to the test area. It sounds like it could provide valuable insights. Additionally, I’m considering implementing the full `State.add` function and possibly scanning types. I'll have to make sure I understand these areas thoroughly, as they seem crucial for the task at hand. Let's see how this all fits together!

**Considering composition requirements**

I think we have enough existing words to work with, but I need to organize the results into specific cases. It’s important to cover various contexts like consonants only, vowels, and combinations of both, while avoiding anything invalid without vowels. I’ll focus on ensuring I include only rising tones and valid existing words. This might require a consultation with a resource to clarify the exact approach, without diving into code just yet. Let’s keep it simple!

content.text: I found the target test is currently a placeholder, and the surrounding tests use table-driven setup of `State.buffer_effective`, direct `State.add`, then assertions on buffer bookkeeping and span fields.

input.task: Critique a test-only approach for the Zig test named exactly "expect State.add apply rising tone for valid cases" in src/liblex.zig. Do not produce any example code, snippets, pseudo-code, or case table syntax. Only discuss coverage strategy and risks in prose.
input.context: Source of truth is specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md. The user wants no code changes and no code output. The test should focus on valid apply-rising-tone cases only: input S/s, existing pseudo-word has at least one vowel, all vowels are level tone, and tone position rules decide where rising tone is applied. It should focus on scan pseudo-word result compositions in the spec: <vowels>, <consonant><vowels>, <vowels><consonants>, <consonant><vowels><consonants>, and avoid invalid/no-vowel/cancel/override/reset cases. Existing tests in src/liblex.zig are table-driven, manually initialize State, set mode=.telex, populate buffer_effective with Span.init or Span.init_diacritic_tone, set buffer_length, call state.add(input), then assert buffer_length unchanged for successful modification, buffer_modification_index equals modified vowel index, literal_index null, mode telex, and check spans preserve base/diacritic/case except changed tone.

run.result:
1) TL;DR

A test-only addition for `expect State.add apply rising tone for valid cases` is a good first move if the goal is to lock the spec before implementation. The main critique is that coverage should be driven by tone-position rule families and scan boundaries, not just by having a few “valid-looking” words; otherwise the test can pass while still missing the real placement logic.

2) Recommended approach (simple path)

1. Keep the scope exactly as narrow as you described.
   Only cover:
   - trigger input `S` and `s`
   - existing buffer in `.telex`
   - pseudo-word has at least one vowel
   - all vowels are level tone
   - result is a successful in-place tone application

   Do not mix in no-vowel, cancel, override, reset, or literal-mode behavior.

2. Organize the single test around the four scan pseudo-word shapes from the spec, not around random Vietnamese examples.
   You want explicit coverage of:
   - `<vowels>`
   - `<consonant><vowels>`
   - `<vowels><consonants>`
   - `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`

   That matters because the scan starts from the end of the buffer, so vowel-ending and consonant-ending words exercise different entry paths.

3. Within those scan-shape groups, cover every tone-position rule family at least once.
   The important families are:
   - single-vowel placement
   - `Ơ` priority
   - ordered priority among `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư`
   - `GI` special handling
   - `QU` special handling when followed by more vowels
   - exact `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` first-vowel rule
   - extended `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` second-vowel rule when followed by more material
   - default first-vowel fallback

4. Treat the spec’s literal families as distinct, not interchangeable.
   `GI` and `QU` should each have their own representative.
   Likewise, `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, and `UY` are discrete spelled-out cases in the spec, so relying on just one of them is weak coverage.

5. For the priority rules, include at least one case that proves ordering, not just presence.
   If every case contains only one “special” vowel, a broken implementation that checks membership but not precedence can still pass. You want at least one valid pseudo-word where two eligible vowels compete and the earlier-priority one must win.

6. Preserve the current assertion style, but make it strict for all spans.
   For each case, the test should verify:
   - `buffer_length` is unchanged
   - `buffer_modification_index` is exactly the chosen vowel index
   - `literal_index` stays `null`
   - `mode` stays `.telex`
   - only one span changes tone
   - the modified span keeps its original base, diacritic, and case
   - every unmodified span remains byte-for-byte as initialized

7. Use both `S` and `s`, but avoid exploding the matrix unnecessarily.
   Since the trigger is case-insensitive, you do not need every word in every trigger-case and every casing permutation. A smaller mix of lowercase, uppercase, and mixed-case seed words is enough to prove case preservation without turning one test into a maintenance problem.

8. Be careful with manually seeded pseudo-words.
   Since the test bypasses normal typing flow and writes spans directly, it is easy to invent sequences that are technically constructible in `Span` but do not actually prove the intended rule. Keep each case clearly tied to one spec clause.

3) Rationale and trade-offs

The simple, test-only approach is reasonable because it creates a spec lock for tone placement without dragging in unrelated behavior. That fits the existing table-driven style in `src/liblex.zig` and keeps the future implementation target crisp.

The main trade-off is false confidence. A test that only samples a few successful words may appear comprehensive but still miss:
- backward scan mistakes
- incorrect priority ordering
- `GI` vs `QU` asymmetry
- the exact-cluster vs extended-cluster split for `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY`

So the right simplification is not “fewer meaningful rule buckets”; it is “one focused test with representative cases per rule bucket.”

4) Risks and guardrails

- Risk: covering scan shapes without covering rule precedence.
  Guardrail: map each case to one specific tone-position clause.

- Risk: overfitting to surface output and missing scanner bugs.
  Guardrail: always assert `buffer_modification_index` and all untouched spans.

- Risk: using only natural-language intuition for syllables.
  Guardrail: trust the spec’s pseudo-word model, especially its scan behavior, more than everyday spelling instincts.

- Risk: accidental combinatorial blow-up.
  Guardrail: use representatives, not full cross-products.

- Risk: one large test becoming hard to debug.
  Guardrail: keep the single required test name, but structure its cases into clearly labeled subgroups by rule family.

5) When to consider the advanced path

Move beyond this single focused test if any of these happen:
- the future tone implementation duplicates logic separately for `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, and `J`
- failures become hard to diagnose because too many rule families are bundled together
- bugs appear around buffer boundaries or literal-mode interactions
- you need confidence in cancel/override/reset interactions, which this test intentionally excludes

6) Optional advanced path

If the tone feature grows, keep this test as the “valid rising apply” spec lock, then add smaller companion tests per rule family or per tone family. That is only worth doing once the implementation exists and debugging granularity starts to matter. Right now, one disciplined, rule-driven test is the simplest good-enough approach.

result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Literal input characters
8: 
9: - When any of the follow characters added to the word, change to literal input from the new character position: `F`, `J`, `W` (ignore cases), because these characters don't appear in formal Vietnamese spelling. `Z` existing in Vietnamese word such as _Dzũng_, _Hồ Dzếnh_
10: - Literal input remains until the new word.
11: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
12: - This rule is lower priority than diacritics and tones rules.
13: 
14: ### Diacritics
15: 
16: Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
17: Tones have a separate section.
18: 
19: #### Empty
20: 
21: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
22: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
23:   characters.
24: 
25: #### Circumflex
26: 
27: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
28: 
29: #### Breve
30: 
31: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
32: 
33: #### Horn
34: 
35: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
36: 
37: #### Stroke
38: 
39: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
40: 
41: #### Apply rules
42: 
43: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
44: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
45:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
46:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
47:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
48: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
49:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
50:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
51:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
52: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
53: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
54: 
55: #### Override rules
56: 
57: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
58:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
59:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
60: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
61: - Keep tone untouched.
62: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
63: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
64: 
65: #### Cancel rules
66: 
67: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
68:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
69: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
70:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
71:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
72:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
73:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
74: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
75: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
76:   remains until the new word.
77:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
78: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
79: 
80: #### Auto-fill rules
81: 
82: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
83:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
84: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
85:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
86:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
87:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
88: - The new input character is appended literally.
89: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
90: 
91: ### Tones
92: 
93: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
94: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
95: These vowel could take any tone.
96: 
97: #### Level (ngang)
98: 
99: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
100:   corresponding variants.
101: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
102: 
103: #### Rising (sắc)
104: 
105: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
106: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
107:   -> `Ớ`.
108: 
109: #### Falling (huyền)
110: 
111: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
112: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
113:   -> `Ờ`.
114: 
115: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
116: 
117: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
118: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
119:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
120: 
121: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
122: 
123: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
124: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
125:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
126: 
127: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
128: 
129: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
130: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
131:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
132: 
133: #### Apply rules
134: 
135: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
136:   character literally.
137: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
138:   their lower cases.
139: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
140: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
141: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
142:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
143: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
144: 
145: #### Cancel rules
146: 
147: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
148:   character literally.
149: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
150:   their lower cases.
151: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
152: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
153: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
154:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
155: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
156:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
157: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
158: 
159: #### Reset rules
160: 
161: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
162:   character literally.
163: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
164: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
165: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
166:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
167: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
168:   any tone (level), append the new character literally.
169: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
170:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
171: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
172: 
173: #### Override rules
174: 
175: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
176:   character literally.
177: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
178:   their lower cases.
179: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
180: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
181: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
182:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
183:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
184: 
185: #### Scan pseudo-word
186: 
187: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
188:   the tone in the correct position.
189: - The existing characters must not be empty.
190: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
191: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
192:   character to scan, then stop.
193: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
194:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
195: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
196:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
197:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
198: 
199: #### Tone position rules
200: 
201: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
202: 
203: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
204:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
205: - Exceptions:
206:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
207: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
208: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
209:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
210:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
211:     prioritize the first to the last in the list.
212:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
213:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
214:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
215:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
216:     consonants, put on second vowel.
217:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
218: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
219: - Could not put tone for other cases.

input.goal: Extract any prior planning or decisions about adding the Zig test named exactly "expect State.add apply rising tone for valid cases" in src/liblex.zig, especially intended coverage boundaries, valid-case-only scope, and assertions to preserve existing span base/diacritic/case while changing only tone.

tool_uses.result:
### Planning for Test: `expect State.add apply rising tone for valid cases` in `src/liblex.zig` 

#### Goal and Scope
The test aims to verify that the `State.add` function correctly applies a rising tone to Vietnamese words that currently have only level tones (no existing tone marks). The source of truth for these rules is `specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md`.

#### Coverage Strategy
The test should focus on valid cases where the input trigger is `S` or `s`. It must cover various "Scan pseudo-word" result compositions as defined in the specification:
- `<vowels>`
- `<consonant><vowels>`
- `<vowels><consonants>`
- `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`

#### Tone Position Rules to Validate
The test must ensure the tone is placed on the correct vowel according to the following priority:
1. **Single vowel**: Tone is placed on that vowel.
2. **Multiple vowels**:
   - Prioritize `Ơ` (and its lower case).
   - Prioritize `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (in that order).
   - **Exceptions**: For words starting with `GI` or `QU` followed by more vowels, the tone is placed on the vowel following `I` or `U`.
   - **Specific clusters**: For `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY`, the tone is placed on the first vowel if they are the only vowels; if followed by more vowels or consonants, it is placed on the second vowel.
   - **Default**: Otherwise, the tone is placed on the first vowel.

#### Test Implementation Pattern
Based on existing conventions in `src/liblex.zig`, the test should use a table-driven approach:
1. **Arrange**: 
   - Manually initialize `State` and set `mode = .telex`.
   - Populate `buffer_effective` with `Span` objects (using `Span.init` or `Span.init_diacritic_tone`) representing a word with `.level` tone.
   - Set the appropriate `buffer_length`.
2. **Act**: Call `state.add('s')` or `state.add('S')`.
3. **Assert**:
   - `buffer_length` remains unchanged (modification of existing span).
   - `buffer_modification_index` matches the index of the vowel where the tone was applied.
   - `literal_index` remains `null`.
   - `mode` remains `.telex`.
   - **Span Fidelity**: Verify that the modified `Span` preserves its original `base` character, `diacritic`, and `case`, with only the `tone` changing from `.level` to `.rising`.

progress.message:
1) TL;DR

A test-only addition for `expect State.add apply rising tone for valid cases` is a good first move if the goal is to lock the spec before implementation. The main critique is that coverage should be driven by tone-position rule families and scan boundaries, not just by having a few “valid-looking” words; otherwise the test can pass while still missing the real placement logic.

2) Recommended approach (simple path)

1. Keep the scope exactly as narrow as you described.
   Only cover:
   - trigger input `S` and `s`
   - existing buffer in `.telex`
   - pseudo-word has at least one vowel
   - all vowels are level tone
   - result is a successful in-place tone application

   Do not mix in no-vowel, cancel, override, reset, or literal-mode behavior.

2. Organize the single test around the four scan pseudo-word shapes from the spec, not around random Vietnamese examples.
   You want explicit coverage of:
   - `<vowels>`
   - `<consonant><vowels>`
   - `<vowels><consonants>`
   - `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`

   That matters because the scan starts from the end of the buffer, so vowel-ending and consonant-ending words exercise different entry paths.

3. Within those scan-shape groups, cover every tone-position rule family at least once.
   The important families are:
   - single-vowel placement
   - `Ơ` priority
   - ordered priority among `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư`
   - `GI` special handling
   - `QU` special handling when followed by more vowels
   - exact `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` first-vowel rule
   - extended `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` second-vowel rule when followed by more material
   - default first-vowel fallback

4. Treat the spec’s literal families as distinct, not interchangeable.
   `GI` and `QU` should each have their own representative.
   Likewise, `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, and `UY` are discrete spelled-out cases in the spec, so relying on just one of them is weak coverage.

5. For the priority rules, include at least one case that proves ordering, not just presence.
   If every case contains only one “special” vowel, a broken implementation that checks membership but not precedence can still pass. You want at least one valid pseudo-word where two eligible vowels compete and the earlier-priority one must win.

6. Preserve the current assertion style, but make it strict for all spans.
   For each case, the test should verify:
   - `buffer_length` is unchanged
   - `buffer_modification_index` is exactly the chosen vowel index
   - `literal_index` stays `null`
   - `mode` stays `.telex`
   - only one span changes tone
   - the modified span keeps its original base, diacritic, and case
   - every unmodified span remains byte-for-byte as initialized

7. Use both `S` and `s`, but avoid exploding the matrix unnecessarily.
   Since the trigger is case-insensitive, you do not need every word in every trigger-case and every casing permutation. A smaller mix of lowercase, uppercase, and mixed-case seed words is enough to prove case preservation without turning one test into a maintenance problem.

8. Be careful with manually seeded pseudo-words.
   Since the test bypasses normal typing flow and writes spans directly, it is easy to invent sequences that are technically constructible in `Span` but do not actually prove the intended rule. Keep each case clearly tied to one spec clause.

3) Rationale and trade-offs

The simple, test-only approach is reasonable because it creates a spec lock for tone placement without dragging in unrelated behavior. That fits the existing table-driven style in `src/liblex.zig` and keeps the future implementation target crisp.

The main trade-off is false confidence. A test that only samples a few successful words may appear comprehensive but still miss:
- backward scan mistakes
- incorrect priority ordering
- `GI` vs `QU` asymmetry
- the exact-cluster vs extended-cluster split for `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY`

So the right simplification is not “fewer meaningful rule buckets”; it is “one focused test with representative cases per rule bucket.”

4) Risks and guardrails

- Risk: covering scan shapes without covering rule precedence.
  Guardrail: map each case to one specific tone-position clause.

- Risk: overfitting to surface output and missing scanner bugs.
  Guardrail: always assert `buffer_modification_index` and all untouched spans.

- Risk: using only natural-language intuition for syllables.
  Guardrail: trust the spec’s pseudo-word model, especially its scan behavior, more than everyday spelling instincts.

- Risk: accidental combinatorial blow-up.
  Guardrail: use representatives, not full cross-products.

- Risk: one large test becoming hard to debug.
  Guardrail: keep the single required test name, but structure its cases into clearly labeled subgroups by rule family.

5) When to consider the advanced path

Move beyond this single focused test if any of these happen:
- the future tone implementation duplicates logic separately for `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, and `J`
- failures become hard to diagnose because too many rule families are bundled together
- bugs appear around buffer boundaries or literal-mode interactions
- you need confidence in cancel/override/reset interactions, which this test intentionally excludes

6) Optional advanced path

If the tone feature grows, keep this test as the “valid rising apply” spec lock, then add smaller companion tests per rule family or per tone family. That is only worth doing once the implementation exists and debugging granularity starts to matter. Right now, one disciplined, rule-driven test is the simplest good-enough approach.

content.text:
No code written.

## Proposed approach for `expect State.add apply rising tone for valid cases`

Use the existing table-driven test style in [`src/liblex.zig`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L936-L979): initialize `State`, set `.telex`, seed `buffer_effective`, set `buffer_length`, call `state.add`, then assert bookkeeping and span details.

The spec constraints for this test should come from the apply-tone, scan, and tone-position sections in [`specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md`](file:///<redacted>/lex/specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md#L135-L219).

### Scope

Keep the test name unchanged:

`expect State.add apply rising tone for valid cases`

Only test successful rising-tone application:

- Trigger input: `S` and `s`.
- Existing buffer is non-empty.
- Existing pseudo-word has at least one vowel.
- All vowels start with `.level` tone.
- Expected result is one existing vowel changing to `.rising`.
- No literal append.
- No cancel, override, reset, no-vowel, or invalid-placeable-tone cases.

The existing no-vowel / unplaceable-tone style is already nearby in [`src/liblex.zig`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L1692-L1781), so this test should not duplicate that behavior.

## Case organization

I would structure the single test around the scan pseudo-word compositions from the spec:

1. `<vowels>`
2. `<consonant><vowels>`
3. `<vowels><consonants>`
4. `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`

I would intentionally exclude `<consonants>` because this test is only valid rising-tone application, and a consonant-only pseudo-word has no placeable vowel.

Within those scan-shape groups, choose representative cases that exercise the tone-position rules:

- Single vowel: tone lands on that vowel.
- Multiple vowels with `Ơ`: tone lands on `Ơ`.
- Multiple vowels with prioritized diacritics: `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư`, including at least one case where more than one priority candidate exists so ordering matters.
- `GI` start with more vowels: `I` is treated as consonant part; tone lands on the next vowel.
- `QU` start with more vowels: `U` is treated as consonant part; tone lands on the next vowel.
- Exact `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY`: tone lands on first vowel.
- Extended `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` with more vowels or consonants: tone lands on second vowel.
- Default fallback: tone lands on first vowel.

I would not cross-product every rule with every composition. That would make the test large without adding much signal. Instead, make sure every scan composition appears, and every tone-position rule family appears at least once.

## Assertion strategy

For each case, assert:

- `buffer_length` is unchanged.
- `buffer_modification_index` equals the exact vowel index that received `.rising`.
- `literal_index` remains `null`.
- `mode` remains `.telex`.
- The target span keeps the same base, case, and diacritic, with only tone changed to `.rising`.
- Every non-target span remains unchanged and `.level`.

That last point matters because a weak implementation could put the tone on the right vowel but accidentally mutate adjacent spans, case, or diacritics.

## Data shape I would use

Conceptually, each case needs:

- Initial spans.
- Trigger character: `S` or `s`.
- Expected modified index.
- A short grouping/comment tying it to the spec clause.

For initial spans:

- Use plain initialized spans for normal letters.
- Use initialized spans with diacritics for `Ơ`, `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư`.
- Keep all initial tones `.level`.

## Oracle perspective

Oracle agreed with the narrow test-only approach and emphasized these points:

- Drive coverage by spec rule families and scan boundaries, not random Vietnamese-looking words.
- Assert `buffer_modification_index` and all untouched spans to catch scanner and placement mistakes.
- Include at least one priority-ordering case, not just single-special-vowel cases.
- Treat `GI`, `QU`, exact `OA`/`OE`/`OO`/`UY`, and extended `OA`/`OE`/`OO`/`UY` as distinct rule families.
- Avoid combinatorial explosion: representatives are better than exhaustive cross-products for this single test.

- About the seeding word. Use real, representative compositions in Vietnamese that could be a real word, a part of the word, a vowels clusters...
- I would intentionally exclude <consonants> -> yes, this is acceptable.
- Include some invalid seeding with 2 separate vowels position, e.g. 'thethuy'. In this case, we expect tone processing still work, the scan will found 'huy' and tone should correctly put on 'u' formed 'thethúy'. Synthesize a few other cases.


Continue the discussion.

content.text:
Agreed. Update the approach this way:

## Revised seeding principle

Use seed buffers that look like real Vietnamese words, word fragments, or valid vowel clusters, instead of abstract letter bags.

The test can still seed `State.buffer_effective` directly, but the case names/comments should describe the intended human-readable composition, for example:

- `a`, `an`, `ba`, `ban`
- `hoa`, `hoan`, `ngoan`
- `huy`, `thuy`
- `gia`, `gian`
- `qua`, `quan`
- `uơ`, `ươ`, `ưa`, `oai`, `uay`, etc. as representative clusters/fragments where applicable

This keeps the test tied to the spec while avoiding unrealistic inputs that obscure why the expected tone position is correct.

## Keep excluding `<consonants>`

Yes. `<consonants>` belongs in invalid/no-placeable-tone tests, not this valid rising-tone test.

For this test, every case should produce a successful in-place `.rising` tone.

## Add “two separate vowel positions” scan-boundary cases

This is important. The scan pseudo-word rule says tone positioning only considers the suffix pseudo-word discovered by scanning backward from the end. So a seed can contain earlier vowels that are outside the scanned pseudo-word, and those earlier vowels must remain unchanged.

Your `thethuy` example is good:

- Seed: `thethuy`
- Final scanned pseudo-word: `huy`
- Shape: `<consonant><vowels>`
- Vowels: `uy`
- Rule: exact `UY` puts tone on first vowel
- Expected conceptual result after `s`: `thethúy`
- Earlier `e` in `the` is ignored by tone placement and remains level.

This is not “invalid” in the sense of impossible buffer data. It is invalid as a single Vietnamese syllable/word, but valid as a scan-boundary stress case.

## Suggested scan-boundary cases

Use a few of these, not too many.

### 1. Earlier vowel before final `huy`

- Seed: `thethuy`
- Scanned pseudo-word: `huy`
- Expected: tone on `u`
- Conceptual result: `thethúy`

Purpose: proves earlier `e` is ignored; exact `UY` rule applies inside the final pseudo-word.

### 2. Earlier vowel before final `hoa`

- Seed: `vanhoa`
- Scanned pseudo-word: `hoa`
- Expected: tone on `o`
- Conceptual result: `vanhóa`

Purpose: proves earlier `a` is ignored; exact `OA` rule puts tone on first vowel.

This is representative because `văn hóa` is real Vietnamese when spaced/toned, and the unspaced buffer is a realistic input-method state.

### 3. Earlier vowel before final `quan`

- Seed: `baoquan`
- Scanned pseudo-word: `quan`
- Expected: tone on `a`
- Conceptual result: `baoquán`

Purpose: proves earlier `ao` is ignored; `QU` at word start treats `u` as consonant part and puts tone on the next vowel.

### 4. Earlier vowel before final `gian`

- Seed: `megian` or `bogian`

Better representative option: use a compound-like or fragment-like prefix if you want it to look less artificial, but the key suffix is `gian`.

- Scanned pseudo-word: `gian`
- Expected: tone on `a`
- Conceptual result: prefix + `gián`

Purpose: proves `GI` at word start treats `i` as consonant part and puts tone on the next vowel.

This case is useful because without the `GI` special rule, an implementation might incorrectly tone `i`.

### 5. Earlier vowel before final `ngoan`

- Seed: `angngoan` is awkward.
- Better: use something like `beongoan` only if you accept phrase-like/fragment-like seeds.

Final suffix:

- Scanned pseudo-word: `ngoan`
- Vowels: `oa`
- Expected: tone on `o`
- Conceptual result: suffix becomes `ngoán`

Purpose: exact `OA` inside `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`.

This may be less necessary if `vanhoa` already covers exact `OA`; include only if you want scan shape with ending consonant.

## Representative valid cases by scan composition

I would organize the test comments/cases like this.

### `<vowels>`

Use vowel-only clusters/fragments:

- `a` → tone on `a`
- `oa` → exact `OA`, tone on `o`
- `uy` → exact `UY`, tone on `u`
- `uoi` / `ươi`-like seeded spans if testing diacritic priority

Purpose: no leading consonant, no trailing consonant.

### `<consonant><vowels>`

Use real syllable-like suffixes:

- `ba` → single vowel, tone on `a`
- `hoa` → exact `OA`, tone on `o`
- `huy` → exact `UY`, tone on `u`
- `gia` → `GI`, tone on `a`
- `qua` → `QU`, tone on `a`

Purpose: most common simple syllable shape.

### `<vowels><consonants>`

Use vowel-starting words/fragments:

- `an` → single vowel, tone on `a`
- `oan` → `OA` starts the vowels and has trailing consonant, so extended `OA` rule puts tone on second vowel: `a`
- `uyen` / `uyn`-style cluster if you want `UY` extended behavior

Purpose: exercises scan when the buffer ends in consonants but the pseudo-word has no leading consonant.

### `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`

Use common syllable-like forms:

- `ban` → single vowel, tone on `a`
- `quan` → `QU`, tone on `a`
- `gian` → `GI`, tone on `a`
- `hoan` → extended `OA` with trailing consonant, tone on `a`
- `thuy` / `huynh` depending on exact cluster desired

Purpose: most important scan shape because it exercises both backward scan paths and tone positioning with trailing consonants.

## Priority cases

Still include a small number of diacritic priority cases using real-looking clusters.

Examples conceptually:

- A syllable containing `ơ` plus another vowel: tone must choose `ơ`.
- A syllable containing multiple priority diacritics, e.g. something with `ê` and `â` if you can make a representative fragment. The point is not that the whole seed is a perfect word; the point is that the vowel cluster is plausible and proves priority ordering.

For these, keep case count low. One `Ơ` priority case and one “priority ordering among special vowels” case is enough for this test.

## Important expectation for multi-vowel-position seeds

For cases like `thethuy`, `vanhoa`, `baoquan`:

- Earlier vowels outside the scanned pseudo-word must remain `.level`.
- Only the vowel selected inside the final scanned pseudo-word changes to `.rising`.
- `buffer_modification_index` must point to that selected suffix vowel, not the earlier vowel.
- `buffer_length` must not increase.
- `literal_index` stays `null`.

These cases are valuable because they verify scan result composition, not just tone-position rules.

## Plan preview: write only test `expect State.add apply rising tone for valid cases`

No production code changes. No test name change.

### Source of truth

Use the tone apply, scan pseudo-word, and tone-position rules from [`specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md`](file:///<redacted>/lex/specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md#L135-L219).

Follow existing test style from [`src/liblex.zig`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L936-L979): table-driven cases, manual `State` initialization, call `state.add`, assert bookkeeping and spans.

## Test structure

Replace the placeholder body of:

[`expect State.add apply rising tone for valid cases`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L1783-L1785)

with one table-driven test.

Each case will describe:

- a short label/comment via case grouping, not necessarily stored in data
- initial seed spans
- input trigger: `S` or `s`
- expected modified index

The test will seed `state.buffer_effective` directly, set `state.buffer_length`, call `state.add(input)`, then assert:

- `buffer_length` unchanged
- `buffer_modification_index == expected_index`
- `literal_index == null`
- `mode == .telex`
- selected span changed to `.rising`
- every other span remains `.level`
- all bases and diacritics preserved

## Scope

Include only valid apply-rising cases:

- trigger is `S` / `s`
- pseudo-word has at least one vowel
- all relevant vowels start level
- tone placement succeeds

Exclude:

- `<consonants>` scan result
- empty buffer
- literal mode
- no-vowel words
- `QU` exactly with no more vowels
- cancel
- override
- reset
- other tones

## Case groups

### Group 1: direct scan compositions

Covers the valid scan result shapes from the spec.

#### `<vowels>`

Representative vowel-only words/fragments/clusters:

- `a` → tone on `a`
- `oa` → exact `OA`, tone on `o`
- `uy` → exact `UY`, tone on `u`

#### `<consonant><vowels>`

Representative syllables/clusters:

- `ba` → single vowel, tone on `a`
- `hoa` → exact `OA`, tone on `o`
- `huy` → exact `UY`, tone on `u`
- `gia` → `GI` start, tone on `a`
- `qua` → `QU` start with more vowel, tone on `a`

#### `<vowels><consonants>`

Representative vowel-starting syllables/fragments:

- `an` → single vowel, tone on `a`
- `oan` → extended `OA` with trailing consonant, tone on `a`
- `uyn` or similar `UY` + trailing consonant fragment → extended `UY`, tone on `y`

#### `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`

Representative syllables:

- `ban` → single vowel, tone on `a`
- `quan` → `QU` start, tone on `a`
- `gian` → `GI` start, tone on `a`
- `hoan` → extended `OA` with trailing consonant, tone on `a`
- `huynh` → extended `UY` with trailing consonants, tone on `y`

### Group 2: priority placement with diacritics

Small focused set to lock priority rules.

- A seed with `ơ` in the scanned vowels → tone on `ơ`.
- A seed with one of `ê`, `â`, `ô`, `ă`, `ư` → tone on the listed priority vowel.
- One seed with multiple priority candidates → confirms ordering, not just “some special vowel gets tone”.

These should still be representative Vietnamese-looking words/fragments, not arbitrary letter bags.

Examples to use if they fit cleanly as span seeds:

- `thuo` seeded as `thươ`/`thưo`-style spans where the intended scanned vowels include `ơ`.
- a fragment like `uê` or a real-looking syllable containing `ê`.
- a constructed but plausible vowel cluster with multiple priority diacritics to prove ordering.

I will keep this group small to avoid an unrealistic table.

### Group 3: scan-boundary / earlier vowel ignored

These are the “invalid as one word, valid as buffer scan stress” cases you requested: seeds with earlier vowel positions before the final pseudo-word. The tone should apply only in the final scanned pseudo-word.

Planned cases:

- `thethuy` → scan suffix `huy`, exact `UY`, tone on `u`; conceptual result `thethúy`
- `vanhoa` → scan suffix `hoa`, exact `OA`, tone on `o`; conceptual result `vanhóa`
- `baoquan` → scan suffix `quan`, `QU`, tone on `a`; conceptual result `baoquán`
- `bogian` or another prefix + `gian` → scan suffix `gian`, `GI`, tone on `a`
- possibly prefix + `hoan` → scan suffix `hoan`, extended `OA`, tone on `a`

For these, assertions must verify earlier vowels remain `.level`.

## Trigger casing

Use both `S` and `s`, but not as a full cross-product for every case. I will distribute them across cases to prove case-insensitive trigger handling without making the test noisy.

## Seeding method

Preferred simplest approach:

- Define a case as an array/slice-like sequence of span descriptors: base + diacritic.
- All initial tones are `.level`.
- During setup, write each seed span into `state.buffer_effective`.
- Set `state.buffer_length` to seed length.
- Expected index tells which span should become `.rising`.

Because `Span` should not be modified directly, the test will create seed spans with `Span.init` / `Span.init_diacritic_tone`, matching repository convention.

## Expected result of the test

The test will initially fail until rising-tone implementation exists, because `S` / `s` currently has an empty branch in `State.add`.

That is acceptable if the current task is only to write the spec-locking test.
